abstract:
In this paper, we introduce the ReferItGame, a novel two-player online game designed to crowdsource and verify natural language referring expressions for objects in real-world images. By framing data collection as a collaborative task, we are able to simultaneously generate descriptions and validate their accuracy through player interaction. To date, this initiative has yielded a large-scale dataset containing 130,525 expressions for 96,654 distinct objects across nearly 20,000 natural photographs.

Our dataset significantly surpasses previous Referring Expression Generation (REG) corpora in both scale and complexity, offering a unique window into how humans describe objects within cluttered, real-world environments. We conduct an extensive analysis of the corpus, uncovering how object categories influence linguistic choices—such as the specific attributes (color, size, location) speakers prioritize and the "entry-level" names they assign to different entities. Drawing on these insights, we propose a new optimization-based model for generating referring expressions that jointly selects attributes and their values. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experimental evaluations across three diverse test sets.
